## Implementation and Training Pipeline

### Configuration-Driven Design

Each experiment is fully specified by an external YAML scenario file covering
data paths, feature subsets, reward type, environment settings, and
optimization hyperparameters. No source-code changes are required to switch
between experimental variants. A pre-training validation stage checks path
availability, feature definitions, split feasibility, and column compatibility
before any computation begins, preventing wasted runs from misconfigured
experiments. A condensed example configuration is provided in Appendix C.

### Feature Pipeline

Feature engineering is registry-driven: feature names in the scenario file
resolve to computation functions, allowing scenario-level composition without
editing training code. This design is central to systematic feature subset
comparisons. Normalization parameters are estimated on the training split only
for non-causal scalers. For the main HFT experiment, features use causal
running normalization: each split is transformed chronologically, and the
normalization state is reset at detected session boundaries. This preserves
causal integrity across both time and the chronological split.

### Training Loop

The processed training split is used to construct the trading environment with
a continuous action space $a_t \in [-1, 1]$ representing target portfolio
exposure. Training follows the TD3 procedure described in Chapter 3, with
comparable implementations for DDPG and PPO for experimental comparison.
All three algorithms share a common training infrastructure: experience
collection, network updates, and evaluation. The primary differences are in the
update rules (deterministic policy gradient for DDPG/TD3, clipped surrogate for
PPO) and replay buffer usage (off-policy for DDPG/TD3, on-policy for PPO).

For TD3 and DDPG, experience is collected into a replay buffer, twin critics are
updated from sampled mini-batches, and the actor is updated every $d$ critic
steps via the deterministic policy gradient. Target networks track the main
networks through soft updates with coefficient $\tau$. Exploration noise is
applied during training and removed at evaluation time. For PPO, training follows
the on-policy paradigm: batches are collected using the current policy, multiple
epochs of updates are performed on each batch, and the clipped surrogate objective
constrains policy changes. The stochastic policy handles exploration through
entropy bonuses rather than explicit noise injection.

```{=latex}
\begin{algorithm}
\caption{TD3 Trading Experiment Workflow}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Experiment configuration (data path, feature set, hyperparameters)
\Ensure Trained policy $\mu_\theta$, evaluation metrics

\State Load and validate configuration; check paths and split feasibility
\State Load L3 order book dataset; sort chronologically
\State Split data into train / validation / test subsets (chronological)
\State Initialise and validate feature pipeline on training split
\State Transform each split chronologically with causal session-aware normalization
\State Build training environment from processed training data
\State Initialise actor $\mu_\theta$, twin critics $Q_{\phi_1}$, $Q_{\phi_2}$,
       target networks $\mu_{\theta'}$, $Q_{\phi_1'}$, $Q_{\phi_2'}$,
       replay buffer $\mathcal{D}$

\For{each step $t = 1, \ldots, \texttt{max\_steps}$}
    \State $a_t \leftarrow \mu_\theta(s_t) + \varepsilon$, \quad $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, \sigma_{\text{explore}})$
    \State Execute $a_t$; observe $r_t$, $s_{t+1}$; store $(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})$ in $\mathcal{D}$
    \State Sample mini-batch $\mathcal{B} \sim \mathcal{D}$
    \State Compute smoothed target action: $\tilde{a}' \leftarrow \mu_{\theta'}(s') + \text{clip}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_{\text{noise}}),\, \pm c\right)$
    \State Compute Bellman target: $y \leftarrow r + \gamma \cdot \min_{i=1,2} Q_{\phi_i'}(s',\, \tilde{a}')$
    \State Update both critics by minimising $\mathcal{L}_{\text{Smooth-L1}}\!\left(y - Q_{\phi_i}(s, a)\right)$
    \If{$t \bmod \texttt{policy\_delay} = 0$}
        \State Update actor: $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \nabla_\theta Q_{\phi_1}(s,\, \mu_\theta(s))$
        \State Soft-update targets: $\phi_i' \leftarrow \tau\phi_i + (1-\tau)\phi_i'$; \; $\theta' \leftarrow \tau\theta + (1-\tau)\theta'$
    \EndIf
    \State Checkpoint and log metrics at configured intervals
\EndFor

\State Evaluate deterministic policy $\mu_\theta$ on test split
\State Compute financial performance metrics from realised return series
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}
```

The notation follows the conventions established in Chapter 3.
$\mu_\theta$ is the actor (deterministic policy) with parameters $\theta$;
$Q_{\phi_i}$ are the two critic networks with parameters $\phi_i$;
primed symbols ($\theta'$, $\phi_i'$) denote the corresponding slowly updated
target networks.
$\mathcal{D}$ is the replay buffer storing past transitions $(s, a, r, s')$.
$\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\text{explore}})$ is the exploration
noise added to actions during training only;
$\sigma_{\text{noise}}$ and $c$ are the target policy smoothing standard
deviation and clip bound respectively.
$\gamma$ is the discount factor, $\tau$ the soft-update coefficient, $\alpha$
the actor learning rate, and $d$ the policy delay (critic updates per actor
update). Concrete values for all parameters are listed in Appendix B.

### Evaluation

Final performance is reported on the held-out test split using a dedicated
evaluation environment. Two output streams are kept strictly separate. First is the
training reward, which serves as an optimization diagnostic. Second is the realized portfolio return
series from which financial metrics are computed.

Conflating these would invalidate
the empirical comparison. For example, treating cumulative DSR as a Sharpe ratio
estimate would be incorrect. Metrics derived from the test rollout are described
in Chapter 6.
